text: Skeletal editing: a surgical approach to structure editing through atom replacement or insertion skeletal editing recently emerged as a new methodology in synthetic chemistry for the design of molecules with minimal changes through the insertion, deletion or replacement of a single atom into, from or within a preformed scaffold that enables the syntheses to be highly economic and for the late stage modification of a lead molecule. Within this review, we provide an overview of the recent advancements within the skeletal editing field with respect to (hetero)cyclic structures and focuses primarily on the single atom editing of a molecule core. A taxonomy of approaches within the skeletal editing has been divided into three categories including ring expansion through atom insertion, ring contraction via atom removal and atom swap transmutations. A perspective that links present day transformations with historical mechanisms that permitted the development of these transformative tools, exemplifying their applicability within the skeleton editing method to explore, in unprecedented detail, a large segment of chemical space and, additionally, elucidates concepts within the relatively new fields of isotopic editing and stereochemical editing; discussing, in addition, challenges that need to be addressed in order for the field of molecular surgery to translate into clinical significance as well as materials advancement.
headline: Fully Human Written
